@moisesmuro4569  Wasn't the point I was trying to make to be honest. The point was that many people don't even understand the complexities of war, expecting wars to be "clean", or "fair" and are assuming that underdogs are always the "good guys".

That's olympic levels of mental gymnastics. 

You never were the good guys. You invaded a sovereign country under false pretenses, and were only out for revenge (and oil). Revenge for the terrorist attacks caused by the very same groups your country trained and armed. There's a litany of war crimes committed by the US army in Iraq. Some of your soldiers butts got (somewhat and understandably) kicked, but is still nothing in comparison to the horrors and damage you've inflicted on the region.

And before you come at me with "yOu doN't kNow nuthin aBoUt wAR", I've been in a real one. Not one fought against some guys in sandals and armed with an AK-47 or an RPG-7.

@lethalchocobo1886 
That's strange, coming from someone who served (Which war were you at? Year? Unit? Rank?), but also literally just built the biggest Straw Man, based on somehow assuming my citizenship, somehow claiming it's MY army, deciding that there was a claim saying that the US Army are somehow the "good guys" and then going and attacking a fruit of their own imagination. Btw, by choosing to attack me, instead of asking questions first, you've rendered yourself guilty of the exact same accusations you made in your Straw Man of an argument.
But allow me to extend an olive branch.
No one is a good guy. It's merely a simplistic, infantile and childish way of addressing conflicts, just like the ridiculous Oppressor vs Oppressed trope. Just because you're claiming you're oppressed and have a chip on your shoulder doesn't give you the right to commit atrocities and justify them.

So how American sniper or lone survivor are true stories, they always add a touch of Hollywood to it to make the film true to life AND entertaining. This movie felt way more raw, these guys were in a shit situation and that’s exactly what the movie conveyed. There was no epic heroics, no glamorous last stand or tantalizing gun battles. It was these famed super soldiers that were outgunned and outmanned and where every other piece of media makes it seem like SEALs are one man armies, this movie made them feel like people.

There really is no plot and unlike its name and the trailer it is not about combat and all it does is portray the horrors of war.

It’s  exhausting how war movies keep portraying American soldiers as heroes who are just saving lives and doing the right thing—when we know that’s not the full story. You don’t get to invade someone’s country, bomb their cities, kill civilians and children, and then act surprised when there’s retaliation. Actions have consequences.

Meanwhile, look at the U.S. today—homelessness is out of control, people are drowning in medical bills, and jobs barely pay enough to survive. How can anyone still see America as a global ‘beacon of hope’ when its own citizens are being neglected?

Let’s be real—the U.S. doesn’t go to war to protect freedom. It goes to secure resources, destabilize regions, and keep feeding the military-industrial complex. Just look at Iraq—there were no weapons of mass destruction. That was a lie. But the oil contracts and defense deals? Very real. War is business. And Hollywood’s role in glorifying it is just part of the machine.

This is the most accurate war film I've ever seen. At the beginning I was giddy ready for an action flick, but by the end of it I was watching it as more of a documentary. Almost like it was filmed during the events. Everything was flawless. It really took me back. The uniforms, the way people behave under stress in combat, the SOPs, the lingo, the behavior, the little details like gear accountability, I mean everything was depicted just like real life. There was no soundtrack. There was no clear cut story. It was just an op gone wrong from start to finish. No nonsense or filler.  This is not Blackhawk Down. This is not Saving Private Ryan. This is a very methodical movie that portrays how combat occurred during the GWOT down to the very minute details.  

Honestly this movie probably won't win any awards, but if you're interested in seeing what the war in Iraq is really like, go see this film. It's solid. I give it a solid 10/10. If you're a vet, go see this if you can handle it. This is a movie by vets for vets.

Sets a new standard for me in terms of sound design. The way the guns sounded real, not just LOUD. I love how they used the different radio channels to imply PoV - you're hearing what the person you're 'riding along with' would have been hearing at that moment. Incredibly well done.
Seems like it makes a big difference having a vet who was actually there co direct and write this movie. Can’t wait to see it!

Just saw this and I couldn't agree more with your comment. Always growing up hearing war stories from service members and older gents from the Korean war I had the honor of speaking with, it really brings to the forefront how ACTUAL war is. You panic (it's an uncontrollable response), you freeze, and you may hesitate when explosions and gunfire are so close to you that your brain and body don't know what to do even for trained people that know what they are about to get into. It's unreal how this film depicts what I can only imagine actual war is like. It's very fucking ugly and inhuman but it gave me so much more respect for war veterans that come back home and even more for those that never come back. The lack of soundtrack and focus on environmental sounds and pain other soldiers are going through really puts you in the moment and gives you a slight glimpse as to what those Men went through.

I 100% agree. You pretty much hit the nail on the head. I was getting flashbacks just watching it. I saw it in the Dolby Cinema where you're literally surrounded by speakers so that added to the overall experience and I actually jumped during the IED scene. The speakers in and around my seat gave me an "Oh shit" kinda feel when the explosion happened which is exactly what it's like to experience it. All in all I hope it gets the recognition that it deserves and is a box office success. It should definitely turn a profit given that the budget doesn't appear to be that much. Lastly, I hope Eric Prydz is grateful for the spike in views and streams for his song Call on Me because I pretty much guarantee that the majority of people leaving the theater looked it up afterwards. I did.

@Mr_Porter The IED scene completely threw me off guard. Literally jumped in my seat and I have no army experience. Cant imagine watching that scene as a veteran. Pure adrenaline.

Just saw the movie in IMAX at an early screening today. 1. Movie was insane when it came to audio and cinematography, watching it at home can be said with literally any movie, just depends on the person who is watching and how they want to experience it when it comes to immersion. 2. Don't understand why politics has to be brought up into a movie about the IRAQ WAR, something that took place 2 decades ago. 3. Movie was based on a real gunfight and based on what they witnessed, once again, during the IRAQ WAR and was directed by an iraq war veteran who was in this specific gunfight shown in the movie. Without spoiling too much, just take this movie as a reenactment because they show the true horrors of war and dont glorify it like other movies do, insane experience to watch it in imax and was a really good movie.
